subject: Physics
q: Two materials having coefficients of thermal conductivity 3K and K and thickness d and 3d respectively, are joined to form a slab as shown in the figure. The temperatures of the outer surfaces are θ2 and θ1 respectively, θ2>θ1. The temperature at the interface is
question: Two materials having coefficients of thermal conductivity 3K and K and thickness d and 3d respectively, are joined to form a slab as shown in the figure. The temperatures of the outer surfaces are θ2 and θ1 respectively, θ2>θ1. The temperature at the interface is
solution:
Let the temperature of the junction T °C .

Rate of heat flow in Rod 1 = rate of heat flow in Rod 2

3kAdθ2-T=kA3dT-θ1

⇒9θ2-T=T-θ1

⇒10T=9θ2+θ1

⇒T=9θ2+θ110=θ110+9θ210

subject: Physics
q: Two metallic blocks M1 and M2 of same area of cross-section are connected to each other (as shown in figure). If the thermal conductivity of M2 is K then the thermal conductivity of M1 will be : [Assume steady state heat conduction]
question: Two metallic blocks M1 and M2 of same area of cross-section are connected to each other (as shown in figure). If the thermal conductivity of M2 is K then the thermal conductivity of M1 will be : [Assume steady state heat conduction]
solution:
Let thermal conductivity of M1 be K'.

Using heat current relation for steady state conduction,

K'A100-8016=KA80-08

⇒K'×2016=K×808⇒K'=8K

subject: Physics
q: The heat radiated per unit area in 1 hour by a furnace whose temperature is $3000 \mathrm{~K}$ is $(\sigma=5.7 \times$ $10^{-8} \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~m}^{-2} \mathrm{~K}^{-4}$ )
question: The heat radiated per unit area in 1 hour by a furnace whose temperature is $3000 \mathrm{~K}$ is $(\sigma=5.7 \times$ $10^{-8} \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~m}^{-2} \mathrm{~K}^{-4}$ )
solution:
According to Stefan's law
$$
E=\sigma T^4
$$
Heat radiated per unit area in 1 hour ( $3600 \mathrm{~s})$ is
$$
=5.7 \times 10^{-8} \times(300)^4 \times 3600=1.7 \times 10^{10} \mathrm{~J}
$$

subject: Physics
q:
A geyser heats water flowing at a rate of 2.0 kg per minute from 30°C to 70°C. If geyser operates on a gas burner, the rate of combustion of fuel will be _____ g min-1.

[Heat of combustion =8×103Jg-1, Specific heat of water =4.2Jg-1C∘-1]
question:
A geyser heats water flowing at a rate of 2.0 kg per minute from 30°C to 70°C. If geyser operates on a gas burner, the rate of combustion of fuel will be _____ g min-1.

[Heat of combustion =8×103Jg-1, Specific heat of water =4.2Jg-1C∘-1]
solution:
Given here, mass flow rate of water, dmdt=2 kg min-1

The geyser heats the water, raising the temperature from θ1=30°C to θ2=70°C.

Total heat utilised in raising the temperature, ΔQΔt=dmdt×sΔθ

ΔQΔt=260×4.2×40=mt×L

L= heat of combination=8×103 J g-1

Now, rate of consumption of fuel is mt=2×4.2×4060×8×103

=4.26×10-3=0.7×10-3 kg sec-1

=42 g min-1

subject: Physics
q: A cylinder of radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> is surrounded by a cylindrical shell of inner radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> and outer radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi></math>. The thermal conductivity of the material of the inner cylinder is <math><msub><mrow><mi>K</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and that of the outer cylinder is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. Assuming no loss of heat, the effective thermal conductivity of the system for heat flowing along the length of the cylinder is:
question: A cylinder of radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> is surrounded by a cylindrical shell of inner radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> and outer radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi></math>. The thermal conductivity of the material of the inner cylinder is <math><msub><mrow><mi>K</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and that of the outer cylinder is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. Assuming no loss of heat, the effective thermal conductivity of the system for heat flowing along the length of the cylinder is:
solution:
Let q1 and q2 be the rate of flow of heat through inner part from outer part respecting net flow of heat


q=q1+q2

=K1πR2l.ΔT+K2.π2R2-R2l.ΔT

=πR2lΔTK1+3K2

If the cylinder is replaced by a single material of thermal conductivity K then

q=K.2R2l.ΔT

On comparison,

K=K1+3K24.

q:
Three containers C1,C2 and C3 have water at different temperatures. The table below shows the final temperature T when different amounts of water (given in liters) are taken from each container and mixed (assume no loss of heat during the process)




































C1 C2 C3 T
1l 2l -- 60oC
-- 1l 2l 30oC
2l -- 1l 60oC
1l 1l 1l θ



The value of θ (in oC to the nearest integer) is___________
question:
Three containers C1,C2 and C3 have water at different temperatures. The table below shows the final temperature T when different amounts of water (given in liters) are taken from each container and mixed (assume no loss of heat during the process)




































C1 C2 C3 T
1l 2l -- 60oC
-- 1l 2l 30oC
2l -- 1l 60oC
1l 1l 1l θ



The value of θ (in oC to the nearest integer) is___________
solution:
Let θ1, θ2 and θ3 be the temperatures of water in three containers.

1θ1+2θ2+0θ3=1+260
θ1+2θ2=180 …. (1)
0×θ1+1×θ2+2×θ3=1+230
θ2+2θ3=90 …… (2)
2×θ1+0×θ2+1×θ3=2+160
2θ1+θ3=180 …. (3)
and θ1+θ2+θ3=1+1+1θ
from (1)+(2)+(3)
3θ1+3θ2+3θ3=450⟹θ1+θ2+θ3=150
from (4) equation 150=3θ⟹θ=50 °C

End of copper rod is maintained at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Where as ends of brass and steel are kept at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Lengths of the copper, brass and steel rods are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>46</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>13</mn><mo> </mo></math>and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cms</mi></math> respectively. The rods are thermally insulated from surroundings except at ends. Thermal conductivities of copper, brass and steel are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>92</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>26</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>12</mn></math> CGS units respectively. Rate of heat flow through copper rod is :
question: Three rods of Copper, Brass and Steel are welded together to form a Y-shaped structure. Area of cross-section of each rod is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. End of copper rod is maintained at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Where as ends of brass and steel are kept at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Lengths of the copper, brass and steel rods are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>46</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>13</mn><mo> </mo></math>and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cms</mi></math> respectively. The rods are thermally insulated from surroundings except at ends. Thermal conductivities of copper, brass and steel are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>92</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>26</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>12</mn></math> CGS units respectively. Rate of heat flow through copper rod is :
solution:
LCu = 46 cm; LB = 13 cm & LS=12 cm.



KCu=0.92KB=0.26KS=0.12}CGSunit

We know that R=LKA

RCu=460.92×4=12.5

RB=130.26×4=12.5

RS=120.12×4=25

Now, if T is temperature of junction then

  100-TRCu=T-0RB+T-0RS

  100-T12.5=T12.5+T25

  ∴100-T12.5=3T25

  200=5T

  ∴T=40∘

⇒QCu=100-TRCu

              =100-4012.5

              =6012.5

  ⇒QCu=4.8 Cal/s

subject: Physics
q: The temperature of a body in air falls from $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $24^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in 4 minutes. The temperature of the air is $16^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The temperature of the body in the next 4 minutes will be:
question: The temperature of a body in air falls from $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $24^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in 4 minutes. The temperature of the air is $16^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The temperature of the body in the next 4 minutes will be:
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \Delta T=T_2-T_1=16^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \\
& \text { And } T_0=16^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \\
& \frac{\Delta T}{t_1}=-k\left(32-16^{\circ}\right)...(i) \\
& \frac{\left(24-T_3\right)}{4}=-k\left(\frac{24+T_3}{2}-16\right) ....(ii) \\
& \frac{16}{4}=-k(16) \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{\left(24-T_3\right)}{4}=-k\left(12+\frac{T_3}{2}-16\right) \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{16}{24-T_3}=\frac{16}{T_3} T_2 \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{T_3}{2}-4=24-T_3 \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{3 T_3}{2}=28 \\
& \Rightarrow T_3=\frac{56}{3}{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}
\end{aligned}$

question: The figure shows a system of two concentric spheres of radii $r_1$ and $r_2$ and kept at temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ respectively. The radial rate of flow of heat in a substance between the two concentric sphere is proportional to

q: Rods $x$ and $y$ of equal dimensions but of different materials are joined as shown in figure. Temperatures of end points $A$ and $F$ are maintained at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ respectively. Given the thermal conductivity of $\operatorname{rod} x$ is three times of that of $\operatorname{rod} y$, the temperature at junction points $B$ and $E$ are (close to):
question: Rods $x$ and $y$ of equal dimensions but of different materials are joined as shown in figure. Temperatures of end points $A$ and $F$ are maintained at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ respectively. Given the thermal conductivity of $\operatorname{rod} x$ is three times of that of $\operatorname{rod} y$, the temperature at junction points $B$ and $E$ are (close to):
solution:
Let $\left[ R = \dfrac{\ell}{3KA} \right]$

$\left[ H = \dfrac{100 - 40}{\dfrac{11R}{2}} \right]$ ...(1)
$H = \dfrac{100 - T_B}{R}$ .....(2)
$H = \dfrac{T_E - 40}{3R}$ .....(3)
using (1) and (2)
$120 = 1100 - 11T_A$
$T_B = 89°C$
using (1) and (3)
$T_E = 73°C$

subject: Physics
q:
A unit scale is to be prepared whose length does not change with temperature and remains 20 cm, using a bimetallic strip made of brass and iron each of different length. The length of both components would change in such a way that difference between their lengths remains constant. If length of brass is 40 cm and length of iron will be _____ cm.

αiron =1.2×10-5 K-1 and αbrass =1.8×10-5 K-1.
question:
A unit scale is to be prepared whose length does not change with temperature and remains 20 cm, using a bimetallic strip made of brass and iron each of different length. The length of both components would change in such a way that difference between their lengths remains constant. If length of brass is 40 cm and length of iron will be _____ cm.

αiron =1.2×10-5 K-1 and αbrass =1.8×10-5 K-1.
solution:
Linear expansion of solid with temperature is given by Lf=L01+α∆T

Here, lB1+αBΔT-li1+αiΔT=lB-li

⇒αBlB=liαi

⇒1.8×10-5×40=li×1.2×10-5

⇒li=1.8×10-5×401.2×10-5=3×402=60

⇒li=60 cm

solution:
dθdt=-σAeMsθ4-θ04

t=-αM4σAeln15T0480T04

subject: Physics
q:
A gas based geyser heats water flowing at the rate of 5.0 litres per minute from $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $87^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The rate of consumption of the gas is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{s}$.
(Take heat of combustion of gas $=5.0 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{g}$) specific heat capacity of water $=4200 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg} \cdot{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
question:
A gas based geyser heats water flowing at the rate of 5.0 litres per minute from $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $87^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The rate of consumption of the gas is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{s}$.
(Take heat of combustion of gas $=5.0 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{g}$) specific heat capacity of water $=4200 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg} \cdot{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
solution:
Flow rate = 5.0 L/min = 5.0 kg/min, $\Delta T = 87 - 27 = 60$°C
Heat required per minute: $Q = mc\Delta T = 5.0 \times 4200 \times 60 = 1.26 \times 10^6$ J/min
If m grams of gas consumed per minute:
$m \times 5.0 \times 10^4 = 1.26 \times 10^6$
$m = \frac{1.26 \times 10^6}{5.0 \times 10^4} = 25.2$ g/min
Rate $= \frac{25.2}{60} = 0.42$ g/s

subject: Physics
q: According to law of equipartition of energy the molar specific heat of a diatomic gas at constant volume where the molecule has one additional vibrational mode is :-
question: According to law of equipartition of energy the molar specific heat of a diatomic gas at constant volume where the molecule has one additional vibrational mode is :-
solution:
A diatomic gas has 5 degrees of freedom apart from vibrational degree of freedom. For each additional vibrational mode, degree of freedom is increased by 2. So new degree of freedom,

f=5+2=7

Therefore, we require molar specific heat capacity at constant volume, which is given by

Cv=f2R=72R

q: Consider a rectangular sheet of solid material of length $\ell=9 \mathrm{~cm}$ and width $\mathrm{d}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$. The coefficient of linear expansion is $\alpha=3.1 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ at room temperature and one atmospheric pressure. The mass of sheet $\mathrm{m}=0.1 \mathrm{~kg}$ and the specific heat capacity $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}=900 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$. If the amount of heat supplied to the material is $8.1 \times 10^2 \mathrm{~J}$ then change in area of the rectangular sheet is :-
question: Consider a rectangular sheet of solid material of length $\ell=9 \mathrm{~cm}$ and width $\mathrm{d}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$. The coefficient of linear expansion is $\alpha=3.1 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ at room temperature and one atmospheric pressure. The mass of sheet $\mathrm{m}=0.1 \mathrm{~kg}$ and the specific heat capacity $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}=900 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$. If the amount of heat supplied to the material is $8.1 \times 10^2 \mathrm{~J}$ then change in area of the rectangular sheet is :-
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \Delta \mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{ms} \Delta \mathrm{T} \\ & 8.1 \times 10^2=0.1 \times 900 \times \Delta \mathrm{T} \\ & \Delta \mathrm{A}=\mathrm{A}_0 2 \alpha \Delta \mathrm{~T}=2.0 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^2\end{aligned}$

q: $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of water is heated from $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Ignoring the slight expansion of the water, the change in its internal energy is (specific heat of water is $4148 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{K}$ ):
question: $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of water is heated from $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Ignoring the slight expansion of the water, the change in its internal energy is (specific heat of water is $4148 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{K}$ ):
solution:
$\Delta \mathrm{Q}=\Delta \mathrm{U}+\Delta \mathrm{W}$ (ignoring expansion)
$\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{ms} \Delta \mathrm{T}=0.1 \times 4.184 \times 20=8.368 \mathrm{~kJ}$

subject: Physics
q: The thermo emf of a thermocouple varies with the temperature $\theta$ of the hot junction as $\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{a} \theta+\mathrm{b} \theta^2$ in volts where the ratio $\mathrm{a} / \mathrm{b}$ is $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If the cold junction is kept at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, then the neutral temperature is
question: The thermo emf of a thermocouple varies with the temperature $\theta$ of the hot junction as $\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{a} \theta+\mathrm{b} \theta^2$ in volts where the ratio $\mathrm{a} / \mathrm{b}$ is $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If the cold junction is kept at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, then the neutral temperature is
options: no neutral temperature is possible for this thermocouple.
solution:
$E=a \theta+b \theta^2$
At neutral temperature $\mathrm{dE} / \mathrm{d} \theta=0$
$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{dE}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}=\mathrm{a}+2 \mathrm{~b} \theta_{\mathrm{n}}=0 ; \theta_{\mathrm{n}}=-\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2 \mathrm{~b}}$
Now $\frac{a}{b}=700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ (given)
$\theta_n=-700 / 2=-350^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
Now $\theta_c=0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
So, $\theta_n>0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
But mathematically $\theta_{\mathrm{n}} < 0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

q: An experiment takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math> to raise the temperature of water in a container from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>0</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>100</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and another <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>55</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math> to convert it totally into steam by a heater supplying heat at a uniform rate. Neglecting the specific heat of the container and taking specific heat of the water to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cal</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>∘</mo></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> , the heat of vaporization according to this experiment will come out to be:
question: An experiment takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math> to raise the temperature of water in a container from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>0</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>100</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and another <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>55</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math> to convert it totally into steam by a heater supplying heat at a uniform rate. Neglecting the specific heat of the container and taking specific heat of the water to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cal</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>∘</mo></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> , the heat of vaporization according to this experiment will come out to be:
solution: Let the constant state of supply of heat per unit time <math><mo>=</mo><mi>λ</mi></math>. So according to the first condition. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>m</mi><mi>c</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mo>⇒</mo><mfrac><mi>λ</mi><mi>m</mi></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>100</mn><mi>C</mi></mrow><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfrac><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>)</mo></math>, and according to the second condition, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>m</mi><msub><mi>L</mi><mi>f</mi></msub><mo>⇒</mo><mfrac><mi>λ</mi><mi>m</mi></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msub><mi>L</mi><mi>f</mi></msub><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac><mo> </mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>ii</mi><mo>)</mo></math>. By <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>ii</mi><mo>)</mo></math> <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>100</mn><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>L</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>⇒</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn><mi>cal</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>100</mn></mrow><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msub><mi>L</mi><mi>f</mi></msub><mn>55</mn></mfrac><mo>⇒</mo><msub><mi>L</mi><mi>f</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>550</mn><mi> </mi><mi>cal</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>

subject: Physics
q: Two different wires having lengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and respective temperature coefficient of linear expansion <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>α</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>α</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo></math> are joined end-to-end. Then the effective temperature coefficient of linear expansion is :
question: Two different wires having lengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and respective temperature coefficient of linear expansion <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>α</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>α</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo></math> are joined end-to-end. Then the effective temperature coefficient of linear expansion is :
solution:
The wire are joined end-to-end, so they are in series combination, then total length of wire is given by
L=L1+L2  ...(1)

When the temperature is increase by ∆θ, then its final length is given by using concept of linear expansion,
L'=L(1+α∆θ)  ...(2)

Using equation (1) and (2) for the series combination of wires, final length is given by

L'=L1'+L2'  ...(3)

⇒L1+L2(1+αeff∆θ)=L1(1+α1∆θ)+L2(1+α2∆θ)

⇒αeff=L1α1+L2α2L1+L2

subject: Physics
q: A liquid in a beaker has temperature $\theta(t)$ at time $t$ and $\theta_0$ is temperature of surroundings, then according to Newton's law of cooling the correct graph between $\log _e\left(\theta-\theta_0\right)$ and $t$ is
question: A liquid in a beaker has temperature $\theta(t)$ at time $t$ and $\theta_0$ is temperature of surroundings, then according to Newton's law of cooling the correct graph between $\log _e\left(\theta-\theta_0\right)$ and $t$ is
solution:
According to Newtons law of cooling.
$\frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto-\left(\theta-\theta_0\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{k}\left(\theta-\theta_0\right)$
$\int \frac{d \theta}{\theta-\theta_0}=\int-k d t$
$\Rightarrow \quad \ln \left(\theta-\theta_0\right)=-k t+c$
Hence the plot of $\ln \left(\theta-\theta_0\right)$ vs $t$ should be a straight line with negative slope.

subject: Physics
q: Water of volume 2 L in a closed container is heated with a coil of 1 kW. While water is heated, the container loses energy at a rate of 160 J/s. In how much time will the temperature of water rise from 27<sup>o</sup>C to 77<sup>o</sup>C ? (Specific heat of water is 4.2 kJ/kg and that of the container is negligible).
question: Water of volume 2 L in a closed container is heated with a coil of 1 kW. While water is heated, the container loses energy at a rate of 160 J/s. In how much time will the temperature of water rise from 27<sup>o</sup>C to 77<sup>o</sup>C ? (Specific heat of water is 4.2 kJ/kg and that of the container is negligible).
solution:
P = 1 0 0 0  watt

∴   Pin=1000-160 J/s=840 J/s

Now,   d Q dt = ms dT dt

∴    840=msdTdt

∴    dt = ms 8 4 0 dT

Integrating both sides we have;

t = ms 8 4 0 ∫ dT

    = 2 × 1 0 0 0 × 1 0 - 6 × 1 0 0 0 × 4.2 × 1 0 0 0 8 4 0 × 5 0

    = 5 0 0 sec

    = 8  min 2 0 sec

question: A bimetallic strip consists of metals A and B. It is mounted rigidly as shown. The metal A has higher coefficient of expansion compared to that of metal B. When the bimetallic strip is placed in a cold both, it will :

αA>αB

q:
The graph between two temperature scales P and Q is shown in the figure. Between upper fixed point and lower fixed point there are 150 equal divisions of scale P and 100 divisions on scale Q. The relationship for conversion between the two scales is given by :

.
question:
The graph between two temperature scales P and Q is shown in the figure. Between upper fixed point and lower fixed point there are 150 equal divisions of scale P and 100 divisions on scale Q. The relationship for conversion between the two scales is given by :

.
solution:
From the graph

For scale P,
Lower fixed point=180-150=30°P

Upper fixed point=180°P

For scale Q,
Lower fixed point=0°Q

Upper fixed point=100°Q

Thus, the relationship between the two scales P and Q is given by

tP-30180-30=tQ-0100-0

⇒tP-30150=tQ100

subject: Physics
q: A block of ice at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is slowly heated and converted to steam at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Which of the following curves represent the phenomenon qualitatively:
question: A block of ice at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is slowly heated and converted to steam at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Which of the following curves represent the phenomenon qualitatively:
solution:
When the state of the substance is not changing, temperature of the substance increases when heat is supplied to it. But when the substance changes the state, the heat supplied to it used as latent heat and the temperature remains constant until all the substance converts to other state.

Therefore, temperature of ice first increases from  -10 °C to 0 °C. Then, remains constant at 0°C till whole ice is melted. Then, it increases from 0 °C to 100 °C. Again it remains constant at 100 °C till whole water converts into steam.

subject: Physics
q: A hole is drilled in a metal sheet. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, the diameter of hole is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. When the sheet is heated to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>177</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, the change in the diameter of hole is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> . The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> will be _____, if coefficient of linear expansion of the metal is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo>/</mo><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mo>∘</mo></mmultiscripts></math>
question: A hole is drilled in a metal sheet. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, the diameter of hole is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. When the sheet is heated to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>177</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, the change in the diameter of hole is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> . The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> will be _____, if coefficient of linear expansion of the metal is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo>/</mo><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mo>∘</mo></mmultiscripts></math>
solution:
Expansion of hole will be similar to that of an imaginary disc.

Let diameter be d0 at 27 °C and d' at 177 °C. Therefore, we can write

d'=d01+αΔT

Therefore, change in diameter will be

⇒d'-d0=5×1.6×10-5×177-27

=12×10-3 cm

Hence, required value of d=12.

subject: Physics
q:
A block of ice of mass 120 g at temperature 0°C is put in 300 g of water at 25°C. The x g of ice melts as the temperature of the water reaches 0°C. The value of x is

[Use: Specific heat capacity of water =4200 J kg-1 K-1, Latent heat of ice =3.5×105 J kg-1]
question:
A block of ice of mass 120 g at temperature 0°C is put in 300 g of water at 25°C. The x g of ice melts as the temperature of the water reaches 0°C. The value of x is

[Use: Specific heat capacity of water =4200 J kg-1 K-1, Latent heat of ice =3.5×105 J kg-1]
solution:
According to the principle of calorimetry, heat lost by hot body is equal to the heat gained by cold body.

The heat released by water

∆Q=ms∆T=0.3×4200×25=31500 J

let m kg ice melts then,

∆Q=mL=m×3.5×105=31500 J

⇒m=31500×10-53.5=9000×10-5

m=0.09 kg=90 g

⇒x=90
